TextENGNew: In researching Hinamizawa Syndrome, there was one critical matter that had to be addressed before anything else.
That matter was the catastrophic collapse that would befall the village should anything happen to the queen carrier.
changeReason: Old says 'If something happens to the queen carrier, the village will collapse' (simple conditional); new properly frames it as 'the catastrophic collapse that would befall the village' as the topic being discussed, matching the JPN nominal structure.

TextENGNew: My grandfather had sounded the alarm about this in his papers, calling it the most dangerous eventuality of all.
According to my grandfather, should the queen carrier die, all ordinary carriers would reach terminal onset within forty-eight hours — based on the average progression time of acute symptoms.
changeReason: Old omits 'based on the average progression time of acute symptoms'; new includes this matching JPN 急性発症の平均的時間から見て, providing important scientific context.

TextENGNew: This may sound outlandish at first, but a careful study of strange local religious collapses and mass suicides recorded throughout the world reveals it as a pattern of truth.
Among the world's inexplicable incidents, a number appear to have been caused by parasitic infections that dominated entire communities — very much akin to Hinamizawa Syndrome.
…Grandfather had singled out cases bearing the closest resemblance to Hinamizawa Syndrome and studied them with meticulous care.
In doing so, he confirmed multiple cases in which the death of a religious leader or figurehead had triggered communal frenzy, ending in total annihilation.
He concluded that the average span for this process was within forty-eight hours.
Indeed, there was no doubt that ordinary carriers were subject to some influence from the queen carrier. Statistical evidence confirmed that the queen's health directly affected the entire village.
Our data had already shown that in any week when Rika fell ill and visited the clinic, the number of other patients arriving was markedly higher than usual.
If even a common cold produced results so dramatic, then it was hardly far-fetched to suppose that something truly dire happening to Rika would bring catastrophe within forty-eight hours.
changeReason: Old says 'we'd be facing a serious situation'; new says 'it was hardly far-fetched to suppose' preserving the logical argument structure of JPN 決して逸脱した話ではなかった (it was not an exaggeration).

TextENGNew: For that reason, the Irie Institute was compelled to hold the protection of queen carrier Rika Furude as a paramount mission — one equal in weight to the research itself.
We had already secured her cooperation as a research subject, but we could not, under any circumstances, allow her life to be placed at risk —
…nor could we permit any malicious party who learned of her existence to take her life.
Her life was, in every sense, the lives of two thousand villagers made one.
And yet.
…And yet, if some accumulation of errors were to result in the fatal outcome — the death of the queen carrier.
changeReason: Old says 'What if something happens to her by accident?'; new says 'if some accumulation of errors were to result in the fatal outcome' -- JPN 何かの間違いが重なり致命的事態として is much more specific about 'accumulation of errors' and 'fatal outcome.'

TextENGNew: …The Irie Institute had to have a response prepared even for that eventuality.
That was why, in the early days of the Institute's establishment, I had drafted an emergency operations manual.
If, by some worst-case chance.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has the following line's content about emergency procedures; JPN simply says もしも万が一 (if by some worst-case chance).

TextENGNew: Should the queen carrier die, or should some other cause trigger mass acute onset among all ordinary carriers—
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has '48-hour emergency procedure'; JPN describes queen carrier death triggering mass acute onset.

TextENGNew: —an emergency measure that must be executed within forty-eight hours of that mass onset.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'prevent damage from spreading'; JPN is about the 48-hour emergency measure.

TextENGNew: ......A last resort to prevent the damage from spreading to neighboring areas.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'plan uses gas, natural disaster'; JPN is about preventing damage from spreading to neighboring areas.

TextENGNew: That was the "Final Operation"—disguised as a natural disaster, it would bury the truth in darkness forever...
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'Alphabet Project took responsibility'; JPN describes the 'Final Operation' disguised as natural disaster.

TextENGNew: The specifics of the operation were handled entirely by my client, the Alphabet Project.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'nuclear and biological weapons'; JPN says the Alphabet Project handled operation specifics.

TextENGNew: As participants in a project accustomed to handling dangerous materials, they were remarkably efficient in everything from planning to organizational structure, and I was immensely grateful.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'execute two thousand villagers'; JPN praises the project participants' efficiency in planning.

TextENGNew: ...An amateur like me could never have devised a plan to deceive the public and erase the "facts," even for a village this small.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'GSDF secured quarry'; JPN says an amateur couldn't devise a plan to deceive the public.

TextENGNew: The GSDF, through a dummy company, acquired a disused quarry in the Yagouchi district upstream of Hinamizawa.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'secret storage base'; JPN describes GSDF acquiring quarry through dummy company.

TextENGNew: This site was designated as a secret stockpile base for operational equipment.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'deadly gas equipment + GSDF destroys'; JPN simply says it was designated a secret stockpile base.

TextENGNew: When the emergency manual was activated, a GSDF special forces unit would use the equipment deployed there to carry out the operation.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'politically dangerous'; JPN describes GSDF special forces executing the operation.

TextENGNew: Yet even the mere act of preparing equipment of such extreme danger carried significant political risk.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old oversimplifies/combines multiple concepts; JPN is specifically about political risk of preparing dangerous equipment.

TextENGNew: To convince them to proceed with preparation regardless, I had to explain the gravely dangerous nature of Hinamizawa Syndrome and make them understand why a contingency response was necessary.
changeReason: Old significantly oversimplifies: reduces a detailed sentence about explaining Hinamizawa Syndrome's danger and contingency necessity to 'prove the need by showing them enough data.'

TextENGNew: I had to demonstrate through documentation just how necessary the Final Operation truly was.
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'grandfather's files come in handy'; JPN is about demonstrating the Final Operation's necessity through documentation.

TextENGNew: For that purpose, the research materials my grandfather had quietly cultivated over so many years proved invaluable…
changeReason: Content misalignment: old has 'clients read grandfather's articles'; JPN says grandfather's long-cultivated research materials proved invaluable.

TextENGNew: The clients read copies of my grandfather's papers and appeared genuinely shaken by the magnitude of catastrophe that Hinamizawa Syndrome could unleash in a worst-case scenario.
changeReason: Old has truncated content 'they were surprised to see how catastrophic things could be' continuing displaced text; new provides the full proper translation of the clients being shaken by the catastrophe potential.

TextENGNew: "…So what you are saying is, if something were to happen to this one girl — the queen — all two thousand-odd villagers would fall into madness…?"
"Yes. In several cases bearing a close resemblance to Hinamizawa Syndrome, mass suicide was not an uncommon outcome —"

"In the case of this syndrome, however, the intense paranoia and heightened sense of crisis could lead to extreme acts of self-defense. We estimate the damage would not be contained to the two thousand carriers alone."
changeReason: Old omits dialogue quote formatting and runs lines together; new properly attributes and translates Takano's statement about mass suicide outcomes.

TextENGNew: "Beginning with anarchic riots, they would give rise to aberrant conditions — grotesque violence, or perhaps something resembling a religious apocalypse."

"If we have no preemptive suppression measures in place, drawing international attention will be unavoidable. And we must bear in mind — from the moment the situation arises, we have only forty-eight hours."
changeReason: Old runs Takano's two dialogue lines together without proper separation; new properly structures them with the crucial 'we have only forty-eight hours' emphasis.

TextENGNew: "…In other words, once it begins, no one can stop it."
"Is it truly impossible to treat all the carriers within forty-eight hours?"
"Out of the question. We have yet to even establish a treatment method, and the Institute is not physically capable of accommodating the entire village."
"…To think there exists a disease as terrifying and strange as this… The world is an incomprehensible place."
``Being prepared at all times for the worst is the essence of national defense — and of the Alphabet Project's founding principles. The goal is not to use this plan. The goal is to have it ready.``
changeReason: Old runs two separate ideas together; new properly separates 'the essence of national defense and the Alphabet Project's principles' from 'The goal is not to use this plan. The goal is to have it ready.'

TextENGNew: "Indeed. Precisely as you say. There are matters regarding the operational guidelines — strengthening the chain of approval authority and so forth — that I would still like to examine, but I understand that preparedness for a worst-case scenario is necessary."
"Let us have the urban-silence warfare research team run a simulation of a silence operation in the Hinamizawa district. I believe this should be carried out without delay."

"Even as we speak, it is possible that the girl — the queen carrier — could be struck by a car and killed at any moment. Thank you, Major Takano. We have further questions, so please, take your seat."
Thanks to a quiet push from one of my backers, the matter was settled without significant resistance.
The briefing consumed nearly an entire day, yet objections were few.
Not one person called my grandfather's papers foolish, or dismissed them with contempt.

…Among the documents I had them reading today were those very papers — the ones that arrogant authorities had once trampled underfoot along with my grandfather's heart, leaving their bootprints behind.
And yet, not a single person here mocked them. They held in their hands what had once been kicked aside, and they read.
It may seem like a small thing, at a glance. But this was the moment my grandfather's research was recognized for the very first time…
Watching them hold my grandfather's documents, point to the pages, and raise them aloft as they debated — I felt something fill my chest to the brim.
…Ah. I understood then — this was what I had been working toward all this time…
Grandpa, up in heaven… can you see this…?
